Appearances:
Plaintiff RAYMOND DIGIACOMO, in pro per present.
Atty. CRAIG ROLFE, Esq. (via Zoom videoconference) of Freeman Mathis & Gary, LLP (tel: 916-472-3300) for Defendants Comcast Of California III, Inc.; Derek B. Nippe; and Dillon N. Auyoung.
Atty. FRANK LAFLEUR, Esq. of The City of San Francisco Attorney's Office (tel: 415-554-3826) for Defendant City and County of San Francisco Attorney's Office.

Hearing Re: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO's Motion For Sanctions Against Comcast.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Raymond DiGiacomo's motion for sanctions against defendant Comcast of California III is denied, but the court sets an order to show cause re defendants' motion for security.

On November 21, 2023, this court entered an order on stipulation between the parties to stay proceedings in this case pending resolution of DiGiacomo v. Recology, Inc., No. CGC-19-581651. On December 19, 2024, the court lifted the stay for the sole purpose of permitting defendants to file motions requiring plaintiff to post security pursuant to CCP 391.1 et seq. Plaintiff's motion to lift the stay was otherwise denied.

The only motion permitted to be filed at present in this case is a motion by a defendant to require plaintiff to furnish security. Plaintiff's motion for sanctions is not such a motion and is therefore denied.

The court recognizes, however, the potential unfairness in an order that gives defendants the unilateral ability to move the case along or let it sit dormant and stayed. The court therefore orders as follows: defendants are ordered to show cause at hearing on July 14, 2025 why the stay should not be lifted. The court expects that defendants will have ample time to file motions for security before then. If they have failed to file, they may address the OSC through a filing at least nine court days in advance of the hearing and Plaintiff may file a responding brief five court days before the hearing.

Counsel for defendant Comcast of California is to prepare and submit a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling with a cover letter demonstrating compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Defendants City And County Of San Francisco, Carla Short And Linda Gerull For A Prefiling Order Pursuant To C.C.P. Sec 391.7 And To Require Plaintiff To Furnish Security Pursuant To C.C.P. Sec 391.1 In The Amount Of $75,000
Off Calendar Oct-06-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

RE: Motion Of Defendants City And County Of San Francisco, Carla Short And Linda Gerull For A Prefiling Order Pursuant To C.C.P. Sec 391.7 And To Require Plaintiff To Furnish Security Pursuant To C.C.P. Sec 391.1 In The Amount Of $75,000.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. The court adopts its tentative ruling. This motion to require a security requires a showing that that the plaintiff is vexatious and that the plaintiff has no reasonable probability of prevailing. The first element is not disputed. 1. The request for judicial notice is not opposed and is granted. 2. The City (and the two individuals represented by the same counsel, together referred to as the City) has not made out the second element because it has not addressed all the claims in the complaint. Its motion briefly discusses the fraud (at least as to C. Short, if no one else) and mandatory duty claims, but not the others. Discussing some of these matters for the first time in a Reply will not do. E.g., High Sierra Rural Alliance v. County of Plumas (2018) 29 Cal.App.5th 102, 111 n.2; Champir, LLC v. Fairbanks Ranch Assn. (2021) 66 Cal.App.5th 583, 591 n.3. (Plaintiff's analysis in his Opposition assumes the City must show there is no reasonable probability of success on every element of the causes of action. That's not true. Showing that one of the necessary elements of a cause of action cannot be established may suffice.) 3. The City does not show how the Franchise Agreement's indemnity provision (Memo at 10:1 ff.) immunizes the City (as opposed to allowing an indemnity claim against Comcast). 4. The evidence relied on by the City comprises the declarations of C. Shortt and L. Gerull. They recite facts on "information and belief," which means they are inadmissible hearsay. Weil & Brown, et al., California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial 8:1104, 9:60 (Rutter: 2023). 5. The proposed security is $75,000. The basis for this is the declaration of F. La Fleur, which recites costs to the City in the range of (for various cases) $2871, $11,000, and up to $88,674. There is no explanation how these numbers suggest $75,000 for the present case. 6. The City's papers, including the La Fleur declaration at 11, and the motion at 11:23-24 (claims brought against the City), suggest that the security is needed to protect against frivolous claims (filed under the Government Code, and as opposed to the filing of suits in Superior Court). It is not clear that granting this motion, or a similar one, can guard against the costs incurred by the City in processing claims. 7. This motion to furnish security is animated by concerns that (a) the City will needlessly incur defense costs in this case and (2) to deter future actions (see Motion at 7:23). As to (a), plaintiff has offered to have this case stayed, and the court encourages the parties (which include parties in addition to the City and plaintiff) to consider a stipulated order to that effect, if that is suitable. As to (b), the court notes that there is now in place a pre-filing order, which was entered July 18, 2023, a few weeks after this case was filed in late June.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Curtis E.A. Karnow. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not Reported. (302/CEK)
